nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers, lakes, and floodplains; sluggish or standing water with abundant cover — submerged roots, overhanging vegetation, and flooded forest margins; often hides in crevices or under debris during the day

decorPreferences: large driftwood and hollow logs for shelter
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
large rounded substrate (sand or fine gravel)
minimal sharp decor (risk of injury to long delicate body)

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in the wild; consumes live fish, frogs, and large invertebrates. In captivity, train onto meaty frozen foods (large silversides, lance fish, prawns, mussel) and high-protein pellets or sticks — the process can take weeks and requires offering food only after lights-out. Live feeder fish accelerate initial acceptance but introduce disease risk. Earthworms and market shrimp are useful transitional foods. Will eat any tankmate small enough to swallow whole.

goodWith: large predatory catfish (Sorubim, Pseudoplatystoma) of similar size
large Gourami species (Giant Gourami) if tank is large enough
large Tinfoil Barbs or similar robust fish too big to swallow
other large Notopterids only if tank is very large (500 L+ per fish) and juveniles introduced together
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be eaten (most community fish)
other knifefish in small tanks (severe conspecific aggression)
Black Ghost Knifefish (Apteronotus albifrons — unrelated family, different care needs, will be eaten or outcompeted)

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species image for Chitala ornata; shows adult lateral profile with characteristic white-ringed black spots on lower body.

summary: A spectacular but impractical aquarium fish reaching up to 100 cm; sold as tiny juveniles yet requires 800 L+ as an adult. Nocturnal piscivore that will eat tankmates. Invasive in Florida (USA) via hobbyist releases. Not recommended for the vast majority of home aquarists.
careNotes: The Clown Knifefish (Chitala ornata) is one of Southeast Asia's largest freshwater fish and one of the most frequently impulse-purchased species that outgrows virtually every home aquarium. Juveniles are sold at 5–10 cm and are undeniably striking, but adults routinely reach 60–80 cm in captivity and can exceed 100 cm in the wild, requiring a tank of at least 800 L with a minimum 240 cm footprint — a setup beyond most hobbyists. Aquarium Co-Op explicitly lists this as a species unsuitable for the hobby for most keepers due to its adult size. It is a dedicated nocturnal predator: it will consume any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth, including other knifefish in smaller tanks. Provide multiple large hiding places (driftwood, large PVC pipes, hollow logs) to reduce stress; dim lighting or floating-plant cover is strongly recommended as the species is photosensitive. Water quality must be pristine — high bioload combined with sensitivity to ammonia spikes makes a large, powerful filtration system essential. Chitala ornata is easily confused with the Royal Clown Knifefish (Chitala blanci), which bears dark vertical stripes instead of spots, and with the completely unrelated Black Ghost Knifefish (Apteronotus albifrons, family Apteronotidae) from South America. Escaped and released C. ornata specimens have established invasive populations in Florida (USA) where the warm climate allows year-round survival; owners who can no longer keep the fish must never release them into the wild.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. In the wild, Clown Knifefish spawn on submerged flat surfaces or broad leaves; males guard the egg mass, fanning it with the anal fin until hatching. Captive spawning has been documented in very large public aquaria and specialized facilities with pairs in tanks exceeding 2,000 L. Hormonal induction has been used in Thailand for aquaculture purposes. Not considered achievable for home hobbyists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Chitala ornata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 100 cm TL), temperature range (24–30°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Chao Phraya and Mekong basins), habitat, diet (piscivore), conspecific behaviour, tank requirements, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Chitala ornata
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Notopteridae, Osteoglossiformes), adult size (reported to 100 cm TL), native range (Indochina: Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, Myanmar), and diet classification (carnivore / piscivore). Also confirms introduction records in Florida, USA.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Clown Knifefish
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide. Notable for explicit 'fish unsuitable for hobby' / extreme-size warning (reaches 3 ft / ~90 cm), minimum 200-gallon (757 L) tank recommendation, nocturnal piscivore feeding notes, and invasive species warning for Florida. Confirms tankmate concerns and difficulty training onto prepared foods.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Clown Knifefish profile
additional.notes: UK trade source; confirmed temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.5–7.5, minimum tank size (very large, 800 L+ implied), care level (expert), and noted species confusion with C. blanci (stripes vs spots) and Apteronotus albifrons (different family, South America).

additional.name: Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission — Clown Knifefish (invasive species)
additional.notes: Documents established feral populations in Florida originating from aquarium releases. Confirms reproduction in South Florida waterways. Used to populate invasive species context in careNotes.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is extremely rare and essentially not achievable in home aquaria; requires very large specialist facilities. Wild-type spawning on flat surfaces with male egg-guarding documented; hormonal induction used in Thai aquaculture.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 80–100 cm; predatory and established invasive in Florida via aquarium releases. Hobby-unsuitable at adult size.
